source_text: “There is a blank left for the surname; and then follows the date; which is within a year before the child was born. I found out that.”
raw_output: “There is a blank left for the surname, and then comes the date, which is within a year before the child was born. I found that out.”
rewrite_text: “There is a blank left for the surname, and then comes the date, which is within a year before the child was born. I found that out.”

source_text: “I’ll lick you to-morrow,” he heard Cheese-Face promise; and he heard his own voice, piping and trembling with unshed tears, agreeing to be there on the morrow.
raw_output: “I’ll lick you to-morrow,” he heard Cheese-Face promise; and he heard his own voice, piping and quavering with unshed tears, agreeing to be there on the morrow.
rewrite_text: “I’ll lick you to-morrow,” he heard Cheese-Face promise; and he heard his own voice, piping and quavering with unshed tears, agreeing to be there on the morrow.

source_text: “No, ma’am; but they be gone to take the places of them who may, which is very close connected. And so I said to myself, Fanny’s young man was one of the regiment, and she’s gone after him. There, ma’am, that’s it in black and white.”
raw_output: “No, ma’am; but they be gone to take the places of them as may, which is very close connected. And so I said to myself, Fanny’s young man was one of the regiment, and she’s gone after him. There, ma’am, that’s it in black and white.”
rewrite_text: “No, ma’am; but they be gone to take the places of them as may, which is very close connected. And so I said to myself, Fanny’s young man was one of the regiment, and she’s gone after him. There, ma’am, that’s it in black and white.”

source_text: We first encode screenplay scenes via a BiLSTM equipped with an attention mechanism. We then contextualize them with respect to the whole screenplay via a second BiLSTM. Next, we compute topic-aware scene representations t i via a context interaction layer (CIL) as proposed in Papalampidi et al. (2019) . CIL is inspired by traditional segmentation approaches (Hearst, 1997) and measures the semantic similarity of the current scene with a preceding and following context window in the screenplay. Hence, the topic-aware scene representations also encode the degree to which each scene acts as a topic boundary in the screenplay. In the final layer, we employ TP-specific attention mechanisms to compute the probability p i j that scene t i represents the j th TP in the screenplay. Note that we expect the TP-specific attention distributions to be sparse, as there are only a few scenes which are relevant for a TP (recall that TPs are boundary scenes between sections). To encourage sparsity, we add a low temperature value τ (Hinton et al., 2015) to the softmax part of the attention mechanisms:
raw_output: We first encode screenplay scenes using a BiLSTM equipped with an attention mechanism. We then contextualize them with respect to the entire screenplay using a second BiLSTM. Next, we derive topic-aware scene representations t i through a context interaction layer (CIL) as introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019). CIL draws inspiration from conventional segmentation methods (Hearst, 1997) and assesses the semantic similarity of the current scene to a preceding and a following context window in the screenplay. Accordingly, the topic-aware scene representations also capture the extent to which each scene functions as a topic boundary in the screenplay. In the final layer, we use TP-specific attention mechanisms to estimate the probability p i j that scene t i corresponds to the j th TP in the screenplay. Note that we expect the TP-specific attention distributions to be sparse, since only a small number of scenes are relevant for a TP (recall that TPs are boundary scenes between sections). To promote sparsity, we incorporate a low temperature value τ (Hinton et al., 2015) into the softmax component of the attention mechanisms:
rewrite_text: We first encode screenplay scenes using a BiLSTM equipped with an attention mechanism. We then contextualize them with respect to the entire screenplay using a second BiLSTM. Next, we derive topic-aware scene representations t i through a context interaction layer (CIL) as introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019). CIL draws inspiration from conventional segmentation methods (Hearst, 1997) and assesses the semantic similarity of the current scene to a preceding and a following context window in the screenplay. Accordingly, the topic-aware scene representations also capture the extent to which each scene functions as a topic boundary in the screenplay. In the final layer, we use TP-specific attention mechanisms to estimate the probability p i j that scene t i corresponds to the j th TP in the screenplay. Note that we expect the TP-specific attention distributions to be sparse, since only a small number of scenes are relevant for a TP (recall that TPs are boundary scenes between sections). To promote sparsity, we incorporate a low temperature value τ (Hinton et al., 2015) into the softmax component of the attention mechanisms:

source_text: One such discrepancy, where strong performance on a standard test set did not correspond to mastery of the task as a human would define it, was documented by McCoy et al. (2019b) for the Natural Language Inference (NLI) task. In this task, the system is given two sentences, and is expected to determine whether one (the premise) entails the other (the hypothesis). Most if not all humans would agree that NLI requires sensitivity to syntactic structure; for example, the following sentences do not entail each other, even though they contain the same words:
raw_output: One such mismatch, in which excellent results on a standard test set did not amount to genuine command of the task in the way a human would characterize it, was reported by McCoy et al. (2019b) for the Natural Language Inference (NLI) task. In this setting, a system is presented with two sentences and must decide whether one sentence, the premise, entails the other, the hypothesis. Most, if not all, humans would concur that NLI demands sensitivity to syntactic structure; for instance, the following sentences do not entail one another, despite sharing the same words:

source_text: Combining matching algorithms Next, we evaluate the ability of our NMT models to combine different matching algorithms. First, we use ⊖(M 1 , M 2 , ...) to denote the augmentation of an input sentence that considers first the match specified by M 1 , if no match applies for the input sentence then it considers using the match specified by M 2 , and so on. Note that at most one match is used. Sentences for which no match is found are kept without augmentation. Similar to Table 2 , models are learned using all the available training data. Table 3 (2 nd block) illustrates the results of this experiment. The first 3 lines show BLEU scores of models combining FM + , NM + and EM + . The last row illustrates the results of a model that learns to use two different matching algorithms. We use the best combination of matches obtained so far (FM + and EM + ) and augment input sentences with both matches. Figure 3 illustrates an example of an input sentence augmented with both a fuzzy match and an embedding match (FM + and EM + ). Notice that the model is able to distinguish between both types of augmented sequences by looking at the token used in the additional stream (factor). As it can be seen in Table 3 (2 nd block), the best combination of matches is achieved by ⊕(FM + ,EM
raw_output: Combining matching algorithms Next, we assess the capacity of our NMT models to combine different matching algorithms. First, we use ⊖(M 1 , M 2 , ...) to denote the augmentation of an input sentence in which the match specified by M 1 is considered first; if no match applies to the input sentence, the match specified by M 2 is then considered, and so forth. Note that no more than one match is used. Sentences for which no match is found are left unaugmented. As in Table 2, the models are trained with all available training data. Table 3 (2 nd block) presents the results of this experiment. The first three lines report BLEU scores for models that combine FM + , NM + and EM + . The final row shows the results of a model that learns to exploit two different matching algorithms. We take the best match combination obtained thus far (FM + and EM + ) and augment input sentences with both matches. Figure 3 shows an example of an input sentence augmented with both a fuzzy match and an embedding match (FM + and EM + ). Note that the model can distinguish between these two types of augmented sequences by examining the token used in the additional stream (factor). As shown in Table 3 (2 nd block), the best match combination is obtained by ⊕(FM + ,EM

Note that no more than one match is used. Sentences for which no match is found are left unaugmented. As in Table 2, the models are trained with all available training data. Table 3 (2 nd block) presents the results of this experiment. The first three lines report BLEU scores for models that combine FM + , NM + and EM + . The final row shows the results of a model that learns to exploit two different matching algorithms. We take the best match combination obtained thus far (FM + and EM + ) and augment input sentences with both matches. Figure 3 shows an example of an input sentence augmented with both a fuzzy match and an embedding match (FM + and EM + ). Note that the model can distinguish between these two types of augmented sequences by examining the token used in the additional stream (factor). As shown in Table 3 (2 nd block), the best match combination is obtained by ⊕(FM + ,EM

source_text: Hidden representations: Recent works (Sun et al., 2019; Romero et al., 2015) have shown the hidden state information from the teacher to be helpful as a hint-based guidance for the student. Given a large collection of task-specific unlabeled data, we can transfer the teacher's knowledge to the student via its hidden representations. However, this poses a challenge in our setting as the teacher and student models have different architectures with disparate output spaces. Consider h s (x k ) and z t l (x k ;θ t ) to be the representations generated by the student and the l th deep layer of the fine-tuned teacher respectively for a token x k . Consider x u ∈ D u to be the set of unlabeled instances. We will later discuss the choice of the teacher layer l and its impact on distillation. Projection: To make all output spaces compatible, we perform a non-linear projection of the parameters in student representation h s to have same shape as teacher representation z t l for each token x k :
raw_output: Hidden representations: Recent studies (Sun et al., 2019; Romero et al., 2015) have demonstrated that the teacher’s hidden-state information can serve as useful hint-based guidance for the student. When a large pool of task-specific unlabeled data is available, the teacher’s knowledge can be transferred to the student through its hidden representations. In our setting, however, this is challenging because the teacher and student employ different architectures and thus produce outputs in different spaces. Let h_s(x_k) and z_t^l(x_k; \theta_t) denote, respectively, the representations produced by the student and by the lth deep layer of the fine-tuned teacher for a token x_k. Let x_u \in D_u denote the set of unlabeled instances. We will later discuss how the choice of teacher layer l affects distillation. Projection: To render all output spaces compatible, we apply a non-linear projection to the parameters in the student representation h_s so that it has the same shape as the teacher representation z_t^l for each token x_k:
rewrite_text: Hidden representations: Recent studies (Sun et al., 2019; Romero et al., 2015) have demonstrated that the teacher’s hidden-state information can serve as useful hint-based guidance for the student. When a large pool of task-specific unlabeled data is available, the teacher’s knowledge can be transferred to the student through its hidden representations. In our setting, however, this is challenging because the teacher and student employ different architectures and thus produce outputs in different spaces. Let h_s(x_k) and z_t^l(x_k; \theta_t) denote, respectively, the representations produced by the student and by the lth deep layer of the fine-tuned teacher for a token x_k. Let x_u \in D_u denote the set of unlabeled instances. We will later discuss how the choice of teacher layer l affects distillation. Projection: To render all output spaces compatible, we apply a non-linear projection to the parameters in the student representation h_s so that it has the same shape as the teacher representation z_t^l for each token x_k:

source_text: Interactive Semantic Parsing Multiple other efforts sought to interactively involve humans in the parsing process itself. He et al. (2016) ask simplified questions about uncertain dependencies in CCG parses and use the answers as soft constraints to regenerate the parse. Both Li and Jagadish (2014) and generate semantic parses and present them in a graphical user interface that humans can control to edit the initial parse. Gur et al. (2018) ask specific predefined multiple choice questions about a narrow set of predefined parsing errors. This interaction model together with the synthetically generated erroneous parses that are used for training can be appropriate for simple text-to-SQL parsing instance as in WikiSQL, which was the only dataset used for evaluation. Yao et al. (2019b) ask yes/no questions about the presence of SQL components while generating a SQL parse one component at a time. Our work falls under the general category of interactive semantic parsing. However, our interaction model is solely based on natural language feedback which can convey richer information and offering a more flexible interaction. Our work is closest to , which also studies correcting semantic parses with natural language feedback, but we (1) focus on text-to-SQL parsing and build on a multi-domain dataset that requires generating complex semantic structures and generalizing to unseen domains (Labutov et al. consider only the domain of email and biographical research);

(2018) ask predefined multiple-choice questions about a narrow set of predefined parsing errors. This interaction model, together with the synthetically generated erroneous parses used for training, may be appropriate for simple text-to-SQL parsing instances such as WikiSQL, which was the only dataset used for evaluation. Our work falls under the broader category of interactive semantic parsing.
